title: Monte Cristo sandwich
subtitle: Ham, turkey, and emmental in an egg-battered sandwich, fried golden
excerpt: Three layers of ham, turkey, and emmental between white bread, dipped in a thin egg-and-milk batter and fried in butter until puffed and golden. Dusted with icing sugar and served with a pot of strawberry jam. Sweet and savoury at the same time, unapologetically so.
sourceNotes: The Monte Cristo is an American adaptation of the French croque-monsieur, filtered through the mid-century diner palate. It appears in American cookery literature by the 1950s and was famously on the menu at Disneyland's Blue Bayou Restaurant from 1966 onward, which gave it widespread American recognition. The fried sandwich technique draws on the French pain perdu tradition, documented in English as 'French toast' since the early eighteenth century.

content.text: The Monte Cristo starts as a ham and cheese sandwich and becomes something else when it hits the batter and the butter. The egg coating puffs slightly as it fries, the bread softens inside while the exterior turns golden and faintly crisp, and the cheese melts into the meat. Dusted with icing sugar and served with jam, the combination of savoury filling and sweet casing is the point, not a fault to be corrected.

The batter should be thin: two eggs and a splash of milk, no more. A thick batter produces a heavy coating that overpowers the filling. The sandwich should be pressed gently before dipping to compact the layers so nothing falls out when you turn it. Use a medium heat to allow the batter to cook through without burning.

items.name: Turkey mince
items.prepNote: use turkey slices rather than mince; sliced cooked turkey breast

content.text: Spread mustard on two of the six bread slices if using. Lay the ham on two slices of bread, then a layer of emmental, then the turkey. Top with a second piece of bread, then the remaining emmental, then a third piece of bread. Press each assembled sandwich firmly with your palm. Cut in half diagonally; press again.

Whisk together the {{eggs}} eggs and {{whole-milk}} of milk with a pinch of salt in a shallow bowl. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in the frying pan over medium heat. Dip each sandwich half briefly in the egg mixture, turning to coat all sides, 10 seconds per side, no longer. Place immediately in the buttered pan. Cook for 2 to 3 minutes per side until golden brown. The cheese should be melted by the time both sides are done.

items.fix: Dip for 10 seconds per side only; the bread should absorb a thin coating, not saturate
items.cause: Too much egg mixture absorbed, or soaked too long
items.symptom: Batter coating is thick and doughy

items.fix: Use medium heat; if the cheese is still solid when the exterior looks done, reduce heat and cover the pan for 1 minute
items.cause: Heat too high; outside cooks before inside warms through
items.symptom: Cheese is not melted when the outside is golden

items.fix: Press the assembled sandwich firmly before dipping and again after coating; use a wide fish slice to support the whole length when turning
items.cause: Not pressed firmly enough before dipping, or layers too loose

items.fix: Taste the filling before assembling; omit extra salt and let the batter be the only seasoned element
items.cause: Ham and cheese are both already seasoned; no need to add salt to the filling

content.text: The Monte Cristo is the American version of the French croque-monsieur. The French press their sandwich with butter and béchamel. The Americans dip theirs in egg batter and serve it with jam. The Disneyland link made the sandwich famous. It first appeared on the Blue Bayou Restaurant menu in the mid-1960s and became one of the most recognised theme-park dishes in the country. It was already a diner staple before that. The sweet finish of icing sugar and jam is not for show. It is the proper American presentation.
